text: The robotic prostatectomy is performed using a transperitoneal approach. The surgeon points to the bladder with the right hand, highlighting the bulge of the Foley catheter balloon. The surgeon begins the posterior approach by cutting the peritoneum beneath the bladder.

The assistant uses a sucker port to provide upwards traction while the surgeon applies downwards traction to mobilize the seminal vesicles and vasa deferentia. The surgeon holds the white tubular structure of the vasa deferentia with the left hand and cuts it, releasing yellowish fluid identified as semen. The surgeon then frees the left seminal vesicle, having already freed the right. The scissors make an incision into the non-VA's fascia, pushing the rectum away from the prostate, which is held above.

The surgeon cauterizes the superficial dorsal vein using the left hand, then cuts and removes it to clear the surgical field. Fat tissue above the prostate is removed to improve visibility, revealing the walnut-shaped prostate with its shiny endopelvic fascia on both sides. The surgeon uses scissors to create a plane between the bladder and prostate, aiming to keep the area dry for better visibility of bladder fibers. As the bladder neck is approached and opened, the yellow Foley catheter becomes visible.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to latch onto the eyelet for traction, while the assistant pulls on the catheter externally, elevating the prostate. The dissection focuses on the posterior wall of the bladder, aiming to create a small bladder neck visible as a pink circular area below the Foley catheter. The surgeon peels and dissects the bladder away from the prostate, separating the anterior and posterior bladder sections.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to elevate the seminal vesicles and vasa, which are visible in the center of the surgical field. The semivascular structures on the right are lifted, followed by those on the left. The surgeon then begins dissecting the pedicles and neurovascular bundles on the left side, applying a permanent clip to the pedicle, which serves as the blood supply to the prostate.

The surgeon places additional clips on the pedicle to minimize back bleeding, using multiple clips depending on the prostate size. The neurovascular bundle is gently dissected away from the prostate to minimize trauma, revealing the shiny white surface of the prostate. The surgeon then ties off the dorsal vein complex, preferring to do this later in the procedure to reduce bleeding and better free the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon ties a slip knot around the dorsal vein complex and secures it with an additional suture, incorporating part of the urethra and anchoring it to the pubic symphysis. The dorsal vein complex is then cut to expose the urethra, with careful dissection to avoid the prostate. The neurovascular bundles are positioned laterally, allowing the use of cautery with cooling irrigation to prevent thermal damage.

The urethra is visible as a tubular structure, with longitudinal fibers coming into view as the prostate tissue is dissected away. The surgeon cuts into the urethra, exposing the Foley catheter, and frees the posterior part of the urethra. The surgical field is clear with minimal bleeding, facilitating visibility during the dissection of recto-urethralis tissue. The prostate is freed and placed in a bag to prevent cancer cell spread. The surgeon begins the anastomosis using quill sutures, treating them like ice picks to expose the bladder mucosa, while the assistant pulls down on the anterior bladder neck.

The surgeon follows the catheter to grasp the posterior urethra, using a twisting motion to bring out the needle. The assistant pushes the catheter in, allowing the surgeon to repeat the steps and secure traction on the urethra with a suture. A six o'clock suture is placed on both the bladder and urethra, and another suture is added to the bladder before pulling the bladder and urethra together.

The surgeon gently tugs to ensure the bladder and urethra are in contact, achieving good apposition. Using a Van Veltoven suture technique, the surgeon stitches from six o'clock to twelve o'clock, bringing the suture through the urethra without needing additional tension support. The process is repeated on the right side to ensure no gaps are left.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis at the twelve o'clock position, using robotic assistance to break the suture. One or two throws are made to secure the suture, followed by testing the anastomosis by filling the bladder with fluid to check for leakage. A drain is placed through a port site to monitor for bleeding or fluid leakage. The abdominal pressure is reduced to check for bleeding, and the robotic instruments are removed as the ports are closed, concluding the procedure.
